data.title: Madison Dividend Income Fund Q1 2026 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
For the first quarter, the Madison Dividend Income Fund (class I) returned +5.8%, which outperformed the S&P 500 Index, Russell 1000 Value Index, and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of -4.3%, +2.1% and +0.2%, respectively.

Throughout 2025, we highlighted in our Dividend Income Investment Strategy Letters what we believed to be growing risks to the overall market due to the combination of S&P 500 valuations near all-time high levels and significant market concentration. Those risks began to play out in the first quarter as valuations contracted. According to FactSet, the expected forward price-to-earnings ratio declined from 22x entering the year down to 18x ending the quarter. Additionally, the eight largest stocks in the S&P 500 (Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta (META), and Tesla (TSLA)), which represented 35% of the index, all underperformed. On a sector basis, the best performing sectors from the prior three years were among the worst performing sectors in the period. Technology, Communication Services, and Consumer Discretionary declined -9%, -9%, and -7%, respectively. These factors caused the S&P 500 to fall -4.4%.

The Dividend Income Fund was insulated from these risks due to its diversified portfolio of cheaper dividend stocks along with the fact it didn't own any of eight largest S&P 500 stocks. In terms of valuation, our preferred metric is looking at the relative yield of dividend stocks versus the S&P 500. Relative yield is defined as a stock or portfolio dividend yield divided by the S&P 500 dividend yield. A high relative yield compared to its long-term history indicates a low valuation for the stock or portfolio, while a low relative yield ratio indicates a high valuation, all else equal. At quarter-end, the relative yield of the Dividend Income Fund was 2.05x the S&P 500 and 1.35x the Russell 1000 Value Index, both at the high end of their historical ranges. In our view, dividend stocks remain on sale compared to both indexes.

Another factor driving Dividend Income outperformance was its large position in Energy, which was the best performing sector by a wide margin, as it gained +38% in the quarter. Notably, none of the other 10 S&P 500 sectors gained more than +10%. The Dividend Income Fund significantly increased its Energy holdings over the past six months by adding to existing positions and starting a new position in ConocoPhillips (COP). Energy increased from 9% of the portfolio on 9/30/2025 up to 16.5% as of 3/31/2026. We believe a new Energy bull market likely began in late 2025 that could continue to drive Energy stock outperformance.

Detracting from relative performance in the first quarter were several of our Financial stocks in what was a weak quarter for the sector.

While overall market valuation levels are cheaper following S&P 500 decline in the quarter, we continue to believe there are risks of further multiple contraction and a market correction. The Dividend Income Fund seeks to protect against these risks by owning a diversified portfolio of high-quality, above-average dividend stocks in cheaper areas of the market. We think Dividend Income has important defensive characteristics that help protect capital in down markets like was experienced in the quarter. These favorable attributes include:

  • An absolute portfolio dividend yield of 2.50%, which compares favorably to the absolute dividend yields of 1.2% for the S&P 500 and 1.85% for the Russell 1000 Value Index as of year-end.
  • A growing income stream that helps protect against inflation. Portfolio holdings have increased their dividends by 6% on average over the past year, which is well above inflation rates over that period. Consistent annual dividend increases are a key metric we require in portfolio holdings.
  • A high-quality portfolio with strong balance sheets that could protect on the downside in a market correction. 94% of fund holdings are rated A- or better by Standard & Poor's, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 at 35% and the Russell 1000 Value at 22%. If there is a down market and/or credit spreads begin to widen, we believe our high-quality portfolio is likely to hold up much better than the overall market.

Performance data shown represents past performance. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate, so that fund shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Past performance does not guarantee future results and current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Visit madisonfunds.com or call 800.877.6089 to obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end.

Summary

The Madison Dividend Income Fund is a conservatively-managed equity strategy that owns a high-quality portfolio of above-average dividend yield stocks with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages (wide moats). We believe that owning high-quality stocks with above-average dividend yields is the best way to provide income and generate attractive long-term returns over a full market cycle while limiting drawdowns in bear markets and market corrections.

Respectfully,

John Brown

Drew Justman

Our expectation is that investors will participate near fully in market appreciation during bull markets and experience something less than full participation during bear markets compared with investors in portfolios holding more speculative and volatile securities. Therefore, the investment philosophy is intended to represent a conservative investment strategy. There is no assurance that Madison's expectations regarding this investment strategy will be realized.

Downside Capture Ratio: a fund's performance in down markets relative to its benchmark. The security's downside capture return is divided by the benchmark's downside capture return over the time period.

Beta: a measure of the fund's sensitivity to market movements. A portfolio with a beta greater than 1 is more volatile than the market, and a portfolio with a beta less than 1 is less volatile than the market.

data.title: BP: The Market Still Underestimates This Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered BP p.l.c. (BP), I rated them Buy, highlighting their smart decision to halt the buybacks as their balance sheet took priority amid the jump in stock price, with the stock returning about 26% since publishing it in February.

Following a solid quarter, BP is reiterated as a Buy given their long-term potential, the near-term Iran boost, and high hopes in their new CEO supporting their turnaround.

Turnaround Advances

BP reported a solid Q1, beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates, although the FCF was negative due to the timing of their working capital build, highlighting the seasonality of the company's inventory builds, being similar in terms of OCF to Q1’25.

Meanwhile, BP also announced the agreement to sell their Gelsenkirchen refinery, which they expect to increase their structural cost reduction target by $1 billion to a massive $6.5 billion to $7.5 billion by 2027, noting that they plan to reduce their hybrid bond financing by about $4.3 billion to $9 billion by the end of 2027, depending on the market conditions.

Due to seasonal maintenance predominantly in the Gulf of Mexico and the effects of disruption in the Middle East, BP expects its Q2 production to drop, while the reported upstream production would be lower in 2026 as a result of the ongoing Iran conflict, or broadly flat on an underlying level.

Regarding the CAPEX, they continue to expect between $13 billion and $13.5 billion (now evenly weighted throughout the year; below the $14.5 billion reported in 2025), expecting between $9 billion and $10 billion in proceeds from divestments this year ($6 billion from Castrol), most of which would come in H2'26, while the Deepwater Horizon would continue to drag them down, as highlighted before.

Financially, based on BP’s latest report, although we see an increase in net debt compared to Q4’25 (still down vs. Q1’25), it’s important to note that given the company’s significant ongoing divestitures and the renewed focus on their balance sheet over buybacks, we’re likely going to see this balance sheet improve significantly over the next couple of years. The company’s main target remains getting to a net debt of $14 billion to $18 billion by the end of 2027 compared to $22.2 billion as of their Q4 report, aiming at improving their credit rating within an A-grade range.

BP also declared a $0.4992 quarterly dividend per ADS, for a yield of roughly 4.30% at the moment of writing this, representing a bit over $5.1 billion and being well covered even if we consider last year’s $9.96 billion free cash flow, leaving plenty of room for them to focus on near-term business improvements before focusing more on growth or higher shareholder returns.

BP’s new CEO, Meg O’Neill, who officially took the helm on April 1, 2026, after her great performance at Woodside Energy (WDS), can also be a major tailwind for the company, with her legacy being really impressive, turning that one into a business with several very solid global operations that are going to fuel their growth for several years ahead, which can also be the case for BP given their portfolio rebalancing mentioned before.

Sure, there are also some shareholders that don’t necessarily want to see a massive swing back to oil and gas; the latest AGM came with over 50% of shareholders voting against the management's plan to ease climate reporting standards, and ~25% voted in favor of demanding more disclosure on capital discipline for fossil fuels.

Iran Boost May Be Temporary

On top of significant improvements in the market’s optimism surrounding BP’s potential turnaround, the Iran conflict offered an extra boost but also another level of risk that investors have to keep in mind.

Although war premiums are likely going to last for months (or more) after the conflict itself is over, that doesn’t mean the price will remain as elevated as today, even though the region’s energy infrastructure has been damaged, oil wells take a lot of effort to reopen once closed (if that’s going to be the case) - although that’s a far bigger issue in the US compared to the Middle East due to the geology - and a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz could be followed by a jump in demand, which combined can lead to a “higher-for-longer” price environment.

This is in line with the latest near-term outlook from the EIA, which sees higher oil prices now persisting into 2027, with significant differences between their February and April outlooks:

We now expect that Brent crude oil prices will increase from an average of $81/b in 1Q26 to a peak of $115/b in 2Q26 before gradually falling to an average of $88/b in 4Q26. We base this on the assumption that the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes but does not return to pre-conflict levels until late 2026. Shut-in oil production gradually returns as flows through the strait resume and oil trade flows adjust. Given this relatively long adjustment period after flows through the strait resume, we expect oil prices will remain elevated, with Brent crude oil prices averaging $76/b in 2027, about $23/b higher than in our February STEO forecast.

Still, we should also consider the effect this event has on the global economy, with expectations of slower GDP growth globally combined with delayed rate cuts or even hikes in some regions.

This can lead to oil’s weak fundamentals from before taking over again once the Iran-driven shock is absorbed, with the potential to see OPEC hike production again to take advantage of the higher prices or more supply hitting the market due to higher near-term profits for oil and gas companies that can be reinvested.

Regarding OPEC - after what happened in Venezuela and Iran - we recently saw news of the UAE, the third-largest producer in OPEC, voluntarily leaving the organization on May 1 after more than 5 decades, which can be a major hit to its impact going forward, while the Iran conflict led to a 27% drop in OPEC's production in March, the biggest hit seen in recent decades. This can also come as a blow to Iran’s proposal to open Hormuz, with WTI jumping above $100 on the news alongside Trump’s negative tone regarding Iran’s plan.

Although a solid boost for the foreseeable future, I believe the Iran conflict and especially the market’s “hysteria” around it can be a threat for BP, its peers, and especially their near-term valuations, and we saw how all of these fell significantly back when the previous truce news came.

Still, given BP’s specific situation, this boost might’ve come at a very good point, potentially boosting the value the company manages to unlock from the assets they want to sell, as they’re technically still in a major asset recycling/divesting cycle. In this regard, I’ll also include the IEA Chief’s warning, who said that the world is facing its biggest energy security threat in history, calling it worse than 1973, 1979, and 2022 combined and stating that it has already changed the fossil fuel industry forever:

Their perception of risk and reliability will change. Governments will review their energy strategies. There will be a significant boost to renewables and nuclear power and a further shift towards a more electrified future.

Risks Reiterated

Although BP’s new CEO can be a major tailwind, and there’s a lot of potential for her to remodel this over the longer term, the Iran conflict adds near-term risk and opportunities we have to pay attention to.

Meanwhile, although this also comes with an opportunity for them to maximize the value they get from their asset sales, there’s still potential to see macro pressure affecting their pivot, while the company and its stock will ultimately always follow the price of the commodity.

Valuation

For BP’s DCF to Equity valuation, assuming oil prices similar to 2025 throughout the entire period (equivalent to a WTI oil price of about $65), given their guidance, lower CAPEX, cost savings, and portfolio adjustments, I’ll assume $12 billion in 2026, followed by a 5% CAGR until 2030, backed by their portfolio’s pivot and further cost/CAPEX reductions, followed by a 3.5% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years but also accounting for a $1 billion boost from 2033 related to their Deepwater Horizon payments dropping and another $300 million added back starting from 2034, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

Note that I’m assuming no change in the company’s net borrowings, as they should be able to repay their debt through the asset sales, and I want to highlight their cash generation potential.

To take into account their specific pivot-related and commodity risks, I’ll use a very high 12% discount rate to demand a strong margin of safety, with 2.582 billion ADS outstanding as of the end of Q1 (or ~15.5 billion for the regular share).

This way, we get to an equity value of $152.77 billion, for an intrinsic value of $9.86 for their regular share and $59.17 per ADS, which remains well above the current $46.57 at the moment of writing this. In other words, even if we assume much more conservative estimates, the current valuation continues to apply a significant margin of safety.

Conclusions

BP remains rated Buy, backed by significant portfolio adjustments and a potential near-term boost from Iran, while their new CEO has the credentials needed to bring them back to their former glory - or better.

There are certainly risks to keep in mind given the fact that we’re talking about a commodity company first and foremost, but the current valuation already prices in significant levels of risk, with solid potential to see the company deliver strong returns if their turnaround goes well.
