data.title: Build-A-Bear Workshop: The Pros And Cons Of Owning The Stock Now
data.description:
Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (BBW) achieved consistent top-line growth in the past few years by managing to turn around a stagnating business. Retail sales increased, international expansion with franchising also gained traction, and the firm's commercial segment also rapidly grew. The macroeconomic headwinds, however, cannot be disregarded - including consumer confidence levels, the tensions in the Middle East, as well as the elevated energy prices. The high short interest also raises concerns. My aim today is to give a balanced view of Build-A-Bear's business and bring you the key reasons why I believe that a "hold" rating is the best I can do at the moment.

Sales

Let me start with sales. Over the past three years, Build-A-Bear managed to increase its sales each year, with retail sales, commercial revenue, and international franchising all growing. There are several key points here, which I would like to highlight:

1. This demonstrates that Build-A-Bear is now a growing business and has a business model that does not only rely on direct-to-consumer sales, but they also managed to diversify their channels. This is largely thanks to the current CEO and her workaround strategy. The issue, however, is that she is set to depart this year, which always introduces some uncertainty about the future of the business.

2. I like that the firm is growing internationally. It is clearly visible from the net retail sales per square foot figures, but also from the amount and distribution of franchise locations. There is one key risk here, however, which I believe is important to consider. Build-A-Bear has 32 franchise locations in the Gulf States, including places like Kuwait, Qatar, and the UAE. Considering the ongoing tension in the region, demand may be soft for BBW's products in these countries in the coming months.

3. Looking forward, the firm does guide for revenue growth, mainly because of new store openings, but it also guides for potentially slightly declining margins. When it comes to topline growth, I generally like to see comparable growth increasing organically and not just by opening new stores.

Comparable growth, however, may face headwinds from consumer confidence and the ongoing tension in the Middle East, as highlighted above.

Further, on the cost side, there may be stronger headwinds than the firm initially accounted for. Tariff headwinds are one thing, which has been explicitly mentioned, but the higher oil prices may last for longer than initially expected. If so, both transportation and manufacturing costs can rise, potentially pressuring Build-A-Bear's margins.

Balance Sheet

Looking at the balance sheet, there are three line items that I would like to discuss. First, the accounts receivable. When talking about the sales, I always like to take a look at how receivables changed in the same period. Generally, accounts receivable changing at the same pace would be desirable. If receivables grow faster than sales, it could be a warning sign that the firm is selling more on credit, potentially pulling demand forward from future periods, or they have changed their accounting. The fact that Build-A-Bear's receivables grew by more than 30% in the prior year may be concerning at first, but it can be explained. The reason that receivables grew fast is a result of the growth of the commercial segment, in which revenue is recognized differently than in the direct-to-consumer business, therefore leading to higher receivables. Regardless, if accounts receivable grow faster than revenue, it still causes cash conversion cycles to lengthen, potentially reducing Build-A-Bear's financial flexibility.

Return to Shareholders

While Build-A-Bear has not been very consistent with paying regular quarterly dividends to their shareholders, they have been committed to returning value. Over the past five years, they used a combination of dividends and share buybacks to return value to investors. In general, I like to see consistency and growth because it allows investors to plan ahead with relatively low uncertainty if they are dependent on these regular payments. But I do understand Build-A-Bear's approach too. They seem to be making payments based on the cash flow generated and the capex used, meaning if they have a higher amount left to pay out, they do that in a relatively flexible manner by buying back shares. It does make planning ahead more uncertain, however. Some may also be concerned by the fact that they use more cash for share buybacks than for dividends; however, this is not an issue from my perspective, as buybacks may be treated in a more attractive way, from a tax point of view, in some jurisdictions.

All in all, I like that Build-A-Bear is committed to returning value to their shareholders, but I would be happier if their dividends or buybacks were more consistent, so planning ahead would be less uncertain.

Valuation

When it comes to the valuation, I do not believe that there is a huge opportunity to take advantage of here. Although Build-A-Bear is selling at a meaningful discount compared to the sector median, it seems to be trading about in line with its historic averages. At this moment, considering the firm's fundamentals and the macroeconomic landscape, I believe Build-A-Bear is fully valued, and some risks may not even be priced in yet, despite the strong price decline year-to-date. At this point, we also have to be aware that the short interest in the stock is almost 21%, which means that in case of a short squeeze, the share price can change rapidly.

All in all, I do not believe that a buy rating could be justified from a valuation point of view.

Conclusions

While Build-A-Bear is a growing business now, it mainly relies on franchising and new store openings to achieve growth. I would much rather see organic, comparable sales growth growing rapidly, driven by strong demand. The macro headwinds, however, are likely presenting this right now, especially consumer confidence levels and the ongoing tension in the Middle East.

The firm has remained committed to returning value to its shareholders; the payments were not really consistent, however. This makes planning ahead difficult, especially for investors, who rely on regular payments.

From a valuation point of view, I am also not too excited about Build-A-Bear, despite the fact that they trade at a discount compared to the sector median. The almost 21% short interest, in fact, raises significant concerns.

For these reasons, I rate Build-A-Bear as a hold now.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: The Foundation For All Things AI
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems Overview

In late October last year, I initiated coverage on Cadence Design Systems, Inc. (CDNS) with a Buy rating. In that analysis, I found signs of robust demand and strong AI tailwinds. While the valuation wasn't cheap, I was bullish as the company played and continues to play a vital role in this technological era. Just earlier this month, the company announced deals with Google Cloud (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA).

As you can see in the chart below, the stock saw a period of weakness after my article was published, but recently there has been a rather sharp rebound. Cadence reported their earnings just yesterday (April 27th) after market close, and so today I have decided to provide an update to see if the investment thesis has changed.

Below, it is shown that demand for Cadence's solutions continues to strengthen. Revenue growth is accelerating, and the backlog has hit a new record. While the fundamentals aren't perfect and the Hexagon deal may cause near-term profitability softness, the long-term outlook seems as strong as ever. With the company being the foundation for the AI revolution, I view the premium valuation as justified and the pullback in the forward P/E since late October as an opportunity to buy. Therefore, I've decided to reiterate my bullish view on Cadence stock.

Demand Remains Robust

Let's start today with a look at their business activity and demand metrics. Firstly, for the top line, Cadence reported revenues worth $1.474 billion. This represents a YoY growth rate of 19%, which is very respectable and is a considerable acceleration from the previous quarter's 6% increase in revenues. This shows strengthening business activity overall, and they also beat analyst expectations by over $18 million to show outperformance.

In their Q1, revenue mix by product category remained unchanged from the year-ago quarter as Core EDA continues to be the flagship offering of the company. In their earnings call, CEO Anirudh Devgan stated that:

As the industry begins transitioning to agentic AI, the need for physically accurate and highly mathematical EDA solutions becomes even more critical.

At this point, it seems that agentic design is becoming increasingly important as the company announced an expanded partnership with TSMC (TSM) just days ago. With agentic AI being a driver for EDA growth, I would say the outlook is strengthening.

Beyond the top line, the company's backlog really speaks volumes regarding how strong demand is right now. They reported that their backlog hit a fresh record of $8.0 billion, and that is up notably from the previous quarter's $7.8 billion and significantly from the prior year period's $6.4 billion. This shows that customer demand continues to pile up.

Furthermore, it should be noted that their cRPOs have increased both QoQ and YoY as well, and so some of this demand should show up on the top line in the not-so-distant future. As a whole, I believe there is little question that current business activity and demand are in favorable positions, with the AI revolution continuing to rely on their solutions for integrated circuit innovation.

Margins A Little Mixed

For Q1, there was a slight mix in how their margins fared, so let's take a look here. As you can see above, non-GAAP gross margin saw 40 basis points of deterioration YoY. Note that in the earnings call, CEO Devgan stated that "demand for our hardware accelerated in Q1, resulting in our best quarter ever" and so it seems that the dip in gross margin may be a result of the evolving software-to-hardware mix.

For costs and expenses, there were generally strong control measures. The $815 million non-GAAP figure represents a 13% increase YoY, which notably lags the top-line growth rate. This is a signal that the business has been overall efficient. This is also signaled in their operating margin. In non-GAAP terms, this key profitability metric expanded by 3 percentage points to 44.7% despite the dip in gross margin. As a whole, while the gross margin dip may not be the best news, I believe it's clear that Cadence remains a very well-run business.

The operating margin strength also trickled its way down to the bottom line. For Q1, the company reported an adjusted EPS of $1.96, up 25% YoY. This outpaces the top line growth rate, represents an acceleration of growth from Q4, and beats analyst expectations by $0.07. Therefore, I believe it would be fair to say that the profitability of their business is currently in fine shape.

Are Cash Flows A Concern?

While the common stock offering of 3.2 million shares back in late February was due to the acquisition of Hexagon AB's design and engineering business, there may be some developments in the company's cash flows and capital returns that are worth mentioning.

Firstly, despite the top and bottom line strength discussed above, operating cash flows dropped by 27% YoY to $356 million. If you take a look at their balance sheet, you'll see that they have net receivables of $1.034 billion as of the end of March. This is a whopping 78% jump YoY, and so the rate at which they are collecting receivables may be weaker than desired. In fact, days sales outstanding (DSO) have increased from 44 in the year-ago quarter to 67.

With CapEx up from the year-ago quarter, FCFs saw a 34% drop. Meanwhile, share repurchases are down YoY from $350 million to $200 million. While it's definitely not time to panic, I believe investors would be wise to keep an eye on their cash flow metrics to see if strength returns, since at the end of the day, cash is king.

2026 Financial Guidance

Cadence's updated outlook now includes the Hexagon design and engineering acquisition that I discussed thoroughly in my previous article. Nonetheless, their midpoint revenue estimate for FY2026 was above consensus expectations, while the adjusted EPS midpoint projection is in line with what analysts were expecting.

As you can see in the table above, revenue growth is now expected to accelerate from FY2025 levels, but it does seem that the Hexagon deal will have unfavorable effects on profitability, at least for the near term. While the top line will strengthen, you can see that non-GAAP operating margin expectations have been lowered and now imply contraction from FY2025.

Also, note that DSO has been revised much higher to 65, and so cash flow weakness may persist. In my previous article, I stated that the Hexagon acquisition may create new opportunities and synergies down the road, and so the short-term pain conveyed by the above guidance may be worth it.

CDNS Stock Valuation Isn't Overly Cheap

In the grand scheme of things, a forward P/E above 40 isn't the cheapest. Seeking Alpha shows that this multiple is at a nearly 80% premium to the IT sector. That said, the current reading of 40.4 is below levels seen in my previous article and also below the average of the past few years. Therefore, I believe there are reasons to believe that Cadence remains a Buy.

To be clear, Cadence is one of the few key companies that have powered the AI revolution beneath the surface. Many of the top chipmakers in the world today use their solutions to drive innovation. In today's article, it was shown that demand is basically at all-time highs, and while their fundamentals aren't perfect (gross margin weakness and cash flow declines), I would say that the company remains incredibly well positioned. Furthermore, the guidance above shows that the Hexagon deal will be a near-term drag on profitability, but at the same time, investors shouldn't forget about the "world-class simulation capabilities" this deal brings, as mentioned in my previous article. Therefore, I believe the long-term outlook for Cadence is as bright as ever right now.

With Cadence being the foundation for all things AI, a premium valuation isn't too surprising in my view. Furthermore, with the valuation now being lower than it was in late 2025 even after the recent rebound, I believe current levels are attractive enough for investors to buy.

Takeaway

My Buy rating for Cadence back in late October hasn't worked out the best, but after today's analysis, I would say that it would be a mistake to give up on the stock now. As stated above, the fundamentals and near-term outlook definitely aren't perfect, but at the end of the day, after the Hexagon deal, Cadence is in a stronger position than ever to provide chipmakers with the solutions that they need to innovate in this AI era. Given their place within the industry and the fact that the valuation is lower than in late 2025, I believe a reiteration of the buy rating would be appropriate now.

data.title: Natural Gas Recovers, But Downside Risks Remain
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

​Henry Hub is currently trading in a weak and relatively narrow range, with the front-month (May 2026, NGK26) holding around $2.50-2.57/MMBtu, with about $2.55 as the nearest short-term reference level. Over the past month the contract has lost roughly 15-20% from its March highs, highlighting pressure from inventories and mild spring weather rather than a clean, sustained downtrend.

The main bearish driver for the front-month is the storage structure: the latest weekly EIA injection of about 103 Bcf and total underground stocks around 2,063 Tcf keep the market in a structurally oversupplied state. This reinforces the seasonal negative bias, turns each EIA report into a trigger for front-month selling, and effectively locks in the “oversupplied spring” phase.

Weather remains the key short-term factor: a mild spring with moderate temperatures is limiting both heating and cooling demand, leaving the front-month in a range-bound regime within roughly $2.36-2.70 and highly sensitive to weather revisions and short-term headlines.

At the same time, the medium-term fundamentals look more balanced. Growing LNG exports to around 17 Bcf/d in 2026, along with further infrastructure expansion, are turning the U.S. into a net gas exporter, pulling volumes out of the domestic market and supporting higher prices further out the curve. Declining production and a concentrated stack of short positions add the risk of a short-covering rally, particularly if the weather deteriorates sharply or a geopolitical trigger appears.

Localized spot weakness, including negative prices in certain regions such as Texas, underscores structural overproduction and infrastructure constraints, but it does not alter the broader fundamental picture already baked into the futures curve.

Traders are increasingly favoring calendar spread strategies (long further-out months vs. short front-month) and positioning for short squeezes in the context of weak weather demand, while keeping short-term directional trades confined within the $2.36-2.70 band and focused on EIA reports and weather updates.

In effect, the current Henry Hub market reflects a clash of two time frames:

  • Fundamentally short-term bearish (front-month suppressed by high inventories and weak demand)
  • Fundamentally medium-term bullish (LNG exports, seasonal patterns, contango, and a structurally rising price profile along the curve)

The market does not look “broken” over the long run; instead, it appears to be passing through a low-cycle phase, after which the expectations of tighter supply and rising demand already embedded in the curve are positioned to materialize in the coming seasons.

The current rebound toward resistance near $2.60 may be used as a selling opportunity, but a confirmed break above that level would open the way for a move toward $2.70.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lotus Creek: A Small Company With Very Experienced Management
data.description:
Lotus Creek Exploration Inc. (LTCEF) aims to be a high-growth company. This is an extremely small company that was spun off from Gear Energy (GENGF), which I covered in past articles. It is actually what was left of Gear Energy after a sale of most of the production to Cenovus Energy (CVE) because Gear had "reached the end of the road" and it was time to transfer that acreage to a larger operation. So, there is a history here under another company name that goes back fairly far. The difference now is the emphasis on light oil and rich gas production.

Special Considerations

The biggest consideration is that this is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars.

Furthermore, when a chart like the above exists, it takes an extreme amount of patience to consider a purchase or sale. Not only is it recommended to use limit orders, but also one needs to have the mindset of waiting the common stock price out. It could easily take months or at least weeks for the stock price to execute at the desired level.

Even though there is more liquidity in Canada, it may still take a fair amount of patience in order to establish a reasonable position.

The last consideration is that this is a company that Don Gray is involved with, along with people who gained experience either at Peyto or Gear Energy. Don Gray is a founder of Peyto and was involved with Gear Energy (the predecessor of this company). Now he is getting older and so is likely less involved with this company than some of the other companies.

But it is important to note that this company has access to people in the companies that he is involved with if those resources are needed. Furthermore, the presence of people like Don Gray, who have built and sold companies before, very often reduces that small company risk that many worry about considerably. While it is still a small company, it is a much safer idea than many companies of comparable size due to the personnel involved.

Because of the experience both on the board and as a part of upper management, things like cash burn and profitability are not the worry that they would be with a regular small company. Here those issues will be controlled much more than one would normally expect.

Noncore Sale

The company sold some noncore acreage with about 300 BOED of probably mostly light oil production for about C$13 million. These proceeds would have taken the long-term debt (which was already low by most standards) down to a very nominal amount.

The company then expanded its capital budget to take into account the low debt levels. Now, if the current period of higher commodity prices lasts, there may be yet another capital budget adjustment.

This company is in the very early growth stages. As a result, there are likely some significant cost savings when the production gets to 10K BOED. Some of that may happen if the purchasing and services occur in conjunction with the related companies that are a part of this group. If indeed it is happening, it is something that is not available to the typical small company.

Budget

This company, as it is currently composed, has only been around for about one year. Because of that, things are likely to change rapidly. This may well limit the usefulness of financial statements. But that makes the evaluation of management extremely important.

The revised guidance includes the sale of noncore assets that closed just after the end of the quarter. What is probably important is that at this early stage, the higher commodity prices may allow for still more activity that would allow for still more growth from a very small base of production.

This organization is not very big on long-term debt. That is especially true for the smaller companies. Therefore, if long-term debt exists at all, that debt ratio will be way below 1.0 (in other words, well within a conservative range).

Accounting

The accounting can have a lot of distortion from the effects of fast growth on an original small base of production. For that reason, it is best to look at "everything" rather than rely on any one statement.

Financially, the net debt is an approximation after the close of the sale of the noncore assets. That figure is expected to fall (probably briefly) to about $600,000. Now the higher-than-expected commodity prices may allow for another well or two to boost the exit production after the Spring Breakup period in Canada that is likely now underway.

Production is way up from the small base upon which production began with the start of the company. At this production level, each well drilled is critically important to total production.

The company does have a few other possibilities. But currently this is the business focus. The live within means focus is very important to this group overall. While there may be some debt incurred, it will not be anything close to the maximum possibility.

Summary

The progress made is shown below. The currently high commodity prices could not have come at a better time. Those high prices enable a period of rapid growth at a time when the company most needs it. A very informal number where some scale cost savings begin is 10,000 BOED. The current commodity price environment may allow the company to get there much sooner than originally anticipated because of more than expected cash flow.

Note the progress made through the end of the first quarter. This is a management that will very carefully manage both costs and cash flow. The debt shown above is before the closing of the noncore acreage. That is different from an earlier slide.

The involvement of some very experienced management makes this a very strong Buy consideration. This is a company that is far more likely to succeed than is the case for many companies with this market value and stock price. The involvement of personnel such as Don Gray (who is a legend in the industry) puts the chances of success in favor of the investor.

I would expect another adjustment to the guidance once things get underway after Spring Breakup (and I would expect that new guidance to adjust upwards as well). The high commodity prices are critical to a company that is really just getting started.

This is a company I would likely hold until the story changes or until the stock price gets too far ahead of itself.

Risks

The stock price itself is so extremely volatile that an investor needs to put up with major changes in value on practically a daily basis. For that reason, I would tend to keep the position very small and make it part of a basket of similarly chosen stocks. A large position in a stock like this could cause a lot of sleepless nights.

The risk of loss of personnel is not quite what it would be at other companies. The reason is that the pool of talent in the whole organization is much larger than would be the case if this company was not associated with the Don Gray group of companies. It is possible that the loss of a key person could set the company back. But it is much less likely when someone can be transferred from a larger company in the group (as is likely to be the case).

The higher commodity prices are likely to get this company off to a much faster start than would normally be the case. A severe and sustained downturn of commodity prices would cause a reevaluation of the outlook. But the conservative debt strategy takes away much of the financial risk that exists elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why The U.S. Fed Is Not Expected To Move On Rates - Yet
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve is widely expected to hold rates steady this week, even as geopolitical risk and rising oil prices complicate the outlook. Oscar Muñoz, Chief U.S. Macro Strategist at TD Securities, joins MoneyTalk to explain why policymakers will likely stand pat—and what could come next as Kevin Warsh’s bid to succeed Jerome Powell as Fed chair appears to face fewer hurdles.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Ahead of the US Federal Reserve rate decision on Wednesday, we've already seen some big news involving the central bank, with the probe into Chair Jerome Powell being dropped by the Department of Justice. Lots going on. Joining us now to discuss is Oscar Munoz, Chief US Macro Strategist with TD Securities. Oscar, great to see you again.

Oscar Munoz: Thanks for the invitation, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: Well, let's start with the news we've had over the past couple of days, the Department of Justice dropping that criminal probe into J. Powell. Does this mean now we have a pretty clear path for Mr. Warsh to take the big chair?

Oscar Munoz: Yeah, certainly, I mean, the dropping of the DOJ investigation is a key development because it essentially advances Warsh's confirmation process. Senator Tillis already announced over the weekend that he will drop his blockade on the process. And we already have a vote from the Senate Banking Committee on Wednesday morning.

The upshot is that it largely secures Warsh will act as Fed Chair for the June FOMC meeting. Different question is, what's Powell going to do? It is up to him to decide if he wants to stay on the board.

Greg Bonnell: That's an interesting thing. Do you think we're going to, perhaps, get some color on that of what Mr. Powell plans on doing? He'll get the questions on Wednesday, no doubt, which looks like it's going to be his last meeting in the big chair. Whether he's going to stick around, you expect much clarity this week?

Oscar Munoz: He'll definitely, 100%, get that question at 2:30 on Wednesday. In the last meeting, he said he hadn't made up his mind. But he made clear that he'll stay as long as the investigations are ongoing.

He wants clarity and finality that all the investigations on the Fed are done. And I don't think we got closure on Friday. So I think he might stick around for a few months to see the whole process through the end. We'll see if he says something on Wednesday because, as I mentioned, I think he'll get that question asked, 100%.

Greg Bonnell: You mentioned that we didn't get full closure. If someone who just looked at the headlines will say, oh, Department of Justice dropped the criminal probe, I guess it's all over. They're still looking at the matter. Just not under a criminal probe.

Oscar Munoz: That's correct. The Fed AG is still taking a look. Jeanine Pirro said that, if necessary, he'll reopen the criminal investigation. So again, it is open ended. It's not completely closed. And that's why we think that Chair Powell might stick around for longer.

Greg Bonnell: All right, so we have a bit of intrigue on that front. We also, of course, heard from Mr. Warsh last week. He was speaking with lawmakers in Washington. Of course, they're grilling him ahead of what kind of Fed Chair he's going to be. Did we get much more color as to what kind of Fed Chair he may be?

Oscar Munoz: I mean, Warsh has made clear that he wants to lower rates. He's not overly preoccupied by the current inflation rate, as he thinks that it is being lifted by temporary factors like tariffs. He's also said that he expects productivity growth to help bring inflation lower, even as the US economy continues to grow strongly. So I think he'll espouse a doubtless bias. But I think it will be hard for him to convince the rest of the committee to ease rates, particularly if inflation remains elevated.

Greg Bonnell: Is that the important factor here, too? We always think about the Fed Chair and the power that they hold. And obviously, you do have the big position on the table. But you're just one vote around that table. So no matter what people might think about his view on rate policy, the other members do have a say.

Oscar Munoz: Certainly, I mean, the Fed is a consensus-based institution. The Fed Chair clearly has priority in setting up the topics and studies. But at the end, there are 12 votes.

And particularly, the makeup of the committee this year is a little bit hawkish. When you think about President Hammack, President Logan, they're not going to ease rates until they see clear evidence of progress on inflation. I think it's going to be really hard to get there by June or Q3. So it was going to be hard for Warsh or any other Fed Chair taking that seat.

Greg Bonnell: When it comes to Wednesday's decision itself, what are you expecting?

Oscar Munoz: Well, apart from the focus on Warsh and whether Powell will stay or not, going back to the economic outlook, I think it's going to be basically a mark to market regarding the Fed's views on how the Middle East conflict and high energy prices are impacting the US economy. We don't think things have changed that much versus the March FOMC meeting. Remember, this is not an SEP meeting, so we're not going to get new updates on the forecast of the economy.

The dot plot is not going to change. We don't expect major modifications to the statement. So we think that most of the focus will be on the Q&A at 2:30. But again, I don't think the Fed can pound the table, in terms of changing policy guidance, because they're still assessing how the economy is absorbing higher oil prices.

Greg Bonnell: Yeah, there's a lot of moving parts there in terms of, can it be inflationary going forward, because, of course, we don't have a resolution to the conflict? Peace process seems a bit stalled at the moment. How is TD Securities seeing it right now as we play through the rest of the year with rate policy? The world is looking for a cut. Are we going to get one this year?

Oscar Munoz: That's our view. We think that there is still room for at least a couple of cuts, one in September, one in December. But that is entirely contingent on getting some progress on inflation.

We think that, over the next few months, we're still going to see month-to-month inflation, being strong on the energy front. Oil prices are going to be elevated, as well. So in the meantime, you're not going to see this push for easing. But we think that changes at the end of Q3, beginning of Q4.

We do expect more progress on the pace of inflation, particularly underlying inflation on a monthly basis. I think that could be enough for the Fed to resume the normalization of policy. But again, it's going to be entirely dependent on how inflation behaves during the rest of the year.

Greg Bonnell: How has the economy been behaving? The general state of the US economy, how would you characterize it?

Oscar Munoz: I think resilient, even though Q1-- we're going to get GDP data the day after the FOMC meeting on Thursday. But if you look at under the hood, when you look at domestic demand, the consumer slowdown in Q1, the main question is, is the consumer going to continue losing momentum in Q2 as inflation starts to impact pockets and spending in real terms? But for now, the consumer is just slowing down, being cautious. The labor market stabilized.

There was a lot of concern toward the end of last year about the unemployment rate increasing closer to 5%. But I think it's fine. Stability around 4.3%, 4.4%, which is where the Fed would like to see the labor market.

Greg Bonnell: Finally, I'll ask you if this does-- if everything goes to plan and this ends up being Jay Powell's last meeting and last decision as Fed Chair, how are the markets, how is history going to remember his tenure as chair?

Oscar Munoz: Yeah, I think he did a good job. I think he was dealt with different unexpected shocks like the pandemic, like tariffs, like the ongoing Middle East conflict. I'd say the only stain on his record, in my view, was that the Fed reacted slowly to the acceleration in prices as the economy reopened post-pandemic in 2021.

But the Fed quickly corrected direction. And I think he's acted courageously against the threat of Fed independence. And I think he will be remembered by the institution in that light.

Original Post

data.title: My Income Portfolio: Dividends Are Forever
data.description:
A Hermitage in a Nutshell

About a kilometer and a half from my house (just under a mile) stands a small church with its rectory and a tiny cemetery surrounded by cypress trees, nestled in a pleasant valley dotted with olive trees. It’s a picturesque rural setting in the hills south of Florence, on the road to the town of Impruneta, the gateway to Chianti.

The complex is entrusted to the Franciscan Sisters of Santa Maria degli Angeli, named after the basilica in Assisi that houses the tiny Chapel of the Porziuncola, where the Saint died in 1226. The two nuns who have lived there for several years have brought it back to life after a long period of neglect, and today it serves as a small hermitage, lovingly cared for and managed by them.

An oasis of peace among the olive trees, with the door left slightly ajar as if to say, “Come on in if you like, you’re welcome here, but please respect the sanctity of the place.” I walk past it with Cesare, my dog, and sometimes we stop on the bench in front of the little cemetery before bringing a carrot to Pongo, a beautiful horse with a golden mane grazing in the olive grove across the road.

My “Thinking Cap”

It is here, during my contemplative walks with a fisherman’s hat on my head—while Cesare sniffs out the scents of wild animals—that my ideas about investments, portfolio strategies, and articles for SA take shape: a bit like Disney’s Gyro Gearloose with his thinking cap.

And, sitting on the bench a few days ago, I was telling myself how great it is to have adopted a dividend strategy, which spares me from having to form an opinion on what the markets will do tomorrow or to forecast how stocks will react to news coming out of Iran or any other corner of the world. While I was in America a month ago, my portfolio dropped to a low of -0.12%, then rebounded to its current level of +6.94%, while in the meantime its securities have consistently paid the distributions and dividends I expected.

In addition, USOI has announced a “coupon” (as the parent company calls it) straight out of One Thousand and One Nights: $7.4743 per share, due to the recent wild swings in oil prices. UTF also announced a dividend adjustment, raising its payout from $0.155 to $0.165 per share.

But let’s look at the dividend history of all the securities in my portfolio, one by one, to appreciate the benefits of this strategy.

A Clash of Cultures

The true investor… will do better if he forgets about the stock market and pays attention to his dividend returns.” (Benjamin Graham)

Of course, I am fully aware of the ongoing debate between those who focus on portfolio growth and those who prioritize the income generated by dividends, which is why I won’t weigh in on the discussion. Instead, I’ll quote John D. Rockefeller, who certainly knew more about the subject than I do: “Do you know the only thing that gives me pleasure? It’s to see my dividends coming in.”

In my case, I aim for both goals with my assets. Today, these consist of a package of 27 securities (18 CEFs, 4 BDCs, 4 ETFs, 1 ETN), divided into three separate portfolios as follows, all of which offer periodic payments:

Cupolone Income Portfolio (named after Brunelleschi’s Florentine dome) consists of fourteen CEFs with monthly distributions:

  • BlackRock Science And Technology Trust (BST)
  • Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II (EOS)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Global Dividend Opps (ETO)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (EVT)
  • Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities (GOF)
  • John Hancock Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (HTD)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Strategy (PCN)
  • Pimco Dynamic Income (PDI)
  • John Hancock Premium Dividend (PDT)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Opportunities (PTY)
  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty (RQI)
  • Special Opportunities Fund (SPE)
  • Cohen & Steers Infrastructure (UTF)
  • Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG)

Giotto Income Portfolio (named after the fourteenth-century Florentine painter and architect) includes four ETFs and one ETN with monthly distributions that adopt covered-call and put-write strategies:

  • JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (JEPI)
  • JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income (JEPQ)
  • UBS ETRACS Crude Oil Shares Covered Call (USOI)
  • WisdomTree Equity Premium Income Fund (WTPI)
  • Global X S&P 500 Covered Call (XYLD)

Masaccio Income Portfolio (named after the founder of Renaissance painting) contains four BDCs and four CEFs with quarterly distributions:

  • Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX)
  • Ares Capital Corp (ARCC)
  • Crescent Capital (CCAP)
  • Fidus Investment Corp (FDUS)
  • Golub Capital (GBDC)
  • Barings Corporate Investors (MCI)
  • Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO)
  • Royce Value Trust (RVT)

As I have reiterated many times in my articles, beyond the payouts, what matters most to me is the long-term NAV trend of my securities, which must be positive to ensure that the distributions and dividends do not erode the value of the underlying assets. Unfortunately, not all of my holdings meet this condition, but the majority show a positive NAV performance since launch –making the payout flow reminiscent of Scrooge McDuck’s iconic cash register noise.

For the sake of convenience, I have used the term “dividend” from now on as an umbrella term for distributions, dividends, interests, or coupons.

Cupolone Income Portfolio

All CEFs included in this portfolio follow a managed distribution policy, which allows them to provide predictable—but not guaranteed—cash flow to common shareholders. Managed distribution plans are policies designed to provide consistent, and when possible increasing, predictable monthly or quarterly cash flow to investors, often aimed at a set percentage of net assets or a fixed dollar amount. In the case of CEFs, distributions can be sourced from net investment income, realized capital gains, and/or return of capital.

BST: The first dividend, of $0.10, was paid in December 2014, and has since risen gradually to the current $0.25, which has been paid since October 2021 (plus some special dividends, the last of which, $1.2015, was paid in December 2025). NAV since launch: +141.3%.

EOS: The first dividend, of $0.144, was paid in March 2005, and since then it has first declined to $0.0875 and then slowly risen to the current $0.1523, which has been paid since April 2024. NAV since launch: +27.80%.

ETO: The first dividend, of $0.10, was paid in June 2004, and since then it has fluctuated over the years, rising and falling several times (plus some special dividends), until reaching the current level of $0.1733, which has been paid since April 2024. NAV since launch: +70.31%.

EVT: The first dividend, of $0.1075, was paid in November 2003, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years (plus some special dividends), reaching the current level of $0.1646, which has been paid since April 2024. NAV since launch: +47.49%.

GOF: The first dividend, of $0.1420, was paid in September 2007, and since then it has slowly increased to the current $0.1821, which has been paid out since May 2013. NAV since launch: –43.30%.

HTD: The first dividend, of $0.0967, was paid in April 2004, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years (plus some special dividends), reaching the current level of $0.158, which will be paid starting in July 2025. NAV since launch: +43.19%.

PCN: The first dividend, of $0.1062, was paid in February 2002, and has then risen to the current $0.1125, which has been paid since May 2012 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: –19.19%.

PDI: The first dividend, of $0.177, was paid in July 2012, and has then risen to the current $0.2205, which has been paid since September 2015 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: –32.55%.

PDT: The first dividend, of $0.075, was paid in January 1995, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years (plus some special dividends), reaching the current level of $0.0825, which has been paid since July 2023 (following a cut from $0.0975). NAV since launch: +32.71%.

PTY: The first dividend, of $0.1375, was paid in February 2003, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years to the current $0.1188, paid starting in September 2021 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: –19.23%.

RQI: The first dividend, of $0.105, was paid in April 2002, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years (with the frequency also varying) to the current $0.09, paid starting in January 2026 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: –9.01%.

SPE: The first dividend, of $0.064, was paid in August 1995, and since then it has risen and fallen several times over the years (with the frequency also varying) to the current $0.1087, paid starting in January 2026 (plus some special dividends, the last of which in December 2025). NAV since launch: +6.13%.

UTF: The first dividend, of $0.085, was paid in May 2004, and since then it has increased several times over the years (with the frequency also varying) to the current $0.165, payable starting in April 2026 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: +49.63%.

UTG: The first dividend, of $0.0967, was paid in April 2004, and has since increased several times over the years to the current $0.20, payable starting in July 2025 (plus some special dividends). NAV since launch: +123.40%.

Giotto Income Portfolio

All ETFs included in this portfolio have a variable distribution. They pay out income –dividends or interest– that fluctuates based on monthly underlying asset performance. As noted, they employ covered-call and put-write strategies.

Over the past five years, JEPI, JEPQ, and XYLD have provided a steady source of distributions derived from Income, while WTPI distributions have included also a certain percentage of Return of Capital in 2023 and 2024. Overall, however, their NAVs have risen (and are today in positive territory since launch,) demonstrating the sustainability of these distributions.

USOI, too, has offered distributions consisting entirely of Income over the same period, despite its decidedly negative NAV performance: personally, I am not overly concerned about this decline — which, incidentally, has rebounded in recent months — given the generous coupons periodically paid out by the security, which this month yielded me nearly 130% on an annualized basis.

JEPI: The first dividend, of $0.4941, was paid in July 2020, and since then it has paid monthly dividends regularly, down to the current $0.4205. NAV since launch: +14.60%.

JEPQ: The first dividend, of $0.3764, was paid in June 2022, and since then it has paid monthly dividends regularly, reaching the current level of $0.5586. NAV since launch: +17.96%.

USOI: The first dividend, of $0.1552, was paid in May 2017, and since then the fund has paid monthly dividends regularly, reaching the current amount of $7.4743. NAV since launch: 89.24%.

WTPI: The first dividend, of $0.6202, was paid in December 2016, and since then it has paid monthly dividends regularly (except in February 2026) up to the current $0.3232. NAV since launch: +31.48%.

XYLD: The first dividend, of $0.0392, was paid in July 2013, and since then it has paid monthly dividends regularly, reaching the current level of $0.3522. NAV since launch: +1.24%.

Masaccio Income Portfolio

Both the four CEFs and the four BDCs included in this portfolio have a variable distribution, on a quarterly basis. Note: For BDCs, price performance is reported rather than NAV performance.

ADX: The first documented dividend, of $0.12, dates to August 1990, and since then it has paid regular dividends, which have grown to the current $0.48. NAV since inception: +168.80%.

ARCC: The first dividend, of $0.30, was paid in December 2004, and since then the company has paid quarterly dividends regularly, up to the current $0.48. Price since launch: +24.30%.

CCAP: The first dividend, of $0.41, was paid in March 2020, and since then the company has paid regular quarterly dividends (interspersed with frequent special dividends) up to the current $0.42. Price since launch: –19.39%.

FDUS: The first dividend, of $0.32, was paid in September 2011, and since then the company has paid regular quarterly dividends (interspersed with occasional special dividends) up to the current $0.43 (+$0.09 special). Price since launch: +22.67%.

GBDC: The first dividend, of $0.24, was paid in June 2010, and since then the company has paid regular quarterly dividends (interspersed with occasional special dividends) up to the current $0.33. Price since launch: +5.77%.

MCI: The first dividend, of $0.95, was paid in July 1989, and since then the fund has paid regular periodic dividends (interspersed with occasional special dividends) up to the current $0.40. NAV since inception: +102.00%.

PEO: The first documented dividend, of $0.20, dates to August 1990, and since then it has paid regular periodic dividends, rising to the current $0.52. NAV since inception: +105.90%.

RVT: The first dividend, of $0.30, was paid in August 1987, and since then the fund has paid regular dividends up to the current $0.30. NAV since inception: +133.20%.

Satisfying in the End

All twenty-seven securities in my portfolio have paid regular dividends since their inception –in some cases for years, and in others for decades– despite all that’s happened over the past quarter-century. Overall, millions of dollars are paid out to shareholders every month, with yields ranging from a modest 5.70% for UTG to a stratospheric 18.90% for GOF (at current market price).

With my purchase price, the overall yield of my portfolio currently stands at just under 10% (excluding USOI’s super-coupon), which translates to a 6.5-7% after taxes. Since many of these are leveraged securities, I’ve had to learn to live with significant volatility over the years, but this hasn’t stopped me from staying the course, even during the toughest times.

Moreover, as we have seen, a significant number of these securities show NAVs that have risen since launch, demonstrating that, in many cases, dividend payments do not erode the underlying asset’s value over the long term. This makes the portfolio’s performance resemble a prolonged rugby play (a sport I love very much), in which the team gains ground by advancing like a tidal wave toward the goal line.

I discovered dividend-paying stocks thanks to a brochure from Franklin Templeton Italia dated March 2011, which promoted two monthly-coupon funds —Global Bond and Global Total Return—and listed among their main benefits the fact that “thanks to the regularity of the monthly coupons, any temporary decline in capital is much easier to bear, as the client still receives a cash flow every month”.

Since then, I’ve made this principle my own and fully embraced a dividend strategy, gradually shifting my focus toward such securities as make up my portfolio. I’ve never regretted it, especially not when I’m enjoying a relaxing moment with my dog on the bench in front of the hermitage on the hill behind my house.

data.title: Alphabet Earnings Preview: Q1 Earnings Hinge On Margins And The Cloud
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Alphabet (GOOGL) is set to step into the earnings spotlight on April 29, and the stakes for the search giant have rarely been higher. While the stock has enjoyed a stellar run to yearly highs, the market’s focus has shifted.

It is no longer enough to simply beat on the top and bottom lines; investors now demand a clear roadmap for how Alphabet’s massive AI investments will translate into durable margin expansion.

With the options market pricing in a 5.63% post-earnings move, well above the 1.44% historical average, volatility is almost guaranteed. Here is what is moving the needle for Alphabet heading into the Q1 print.

The Fundamental Backdrop: Revenue Resilience

Wall Street remains optimistic about the core engine. Consensus estimates peg revenue at $106.89 billion, a 19% year-over-year jump.

However, the earnings per share (EPS) outlook is more nuanced, with estimates ranging from $2.10 to $2.68.

The lower end of that EPS range reflects the "AI tax", the heavy infrastructure costs Alphabet is absorbing to keep pace in the generative AI arms race. The market will be hypersensitive to any signs that these costs are beginning to erode the company's historically robust operating margins.

Key Swing Factors for Market Participants

The Cloud as a Success Barometer

Google Cloud is the primary battlefield. Analysts are looking for 50% year-over-year growth, but the real "tell" will be the Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO). A surge in RPO would confirm that enterprise clients are locking into long-term AI contracts, justifying the current valuation. If cloud growth stalls while capital expenditure climbs, expect the "spending ahead of demand" narrative to weigh heavily on the share price.

The Capex Trajectory

Alphabet has telegraphed a staggering $75 billion 2026 investment plan. Management needs to strike a delicate balance: reassure the market that they aren't falling behind Microsoft (MSFT) or OpenAI (OPENAI), while proving that they aren't overbuilding capacity. A capex raise without a matching revenue guide-up is a recipe for a post-earnings sell-off.

Strategic Wins

The Pentagon AI Deal: A significant tailwind heading into this release is the reported classified Pentagon AI deal. By securing a spot alongside OpenAI and xAI in defense infrastructure, Google has secured a massive "trust" win. This deal serves as a powerful rebuttal to critics who questioned Google’s ability to compete in high-stakes, secure AI environments.

Technical Outlook and Potential Implications

From a technical perspective, GOOGL has been a "Strong Buy" darling, but the risk of a "sell the news" event is elevated at these levels.

  • The Bull Case: A "clean" beat characterized by cloud acceleration and stable margins would likely see Alphabet test the $400 psychological level, supported by a flurry of analyst upgrades.
  • The Bear Case: Any weakness in YouTube advertising or a dip in operating margins toward the 31% mark could trigger a swift derating. In this scenario, the stock may look to find support at previous consolidation zones as the market re-evaluates the ROI on AI.

Key Levels to Watch:

316.26 (100-day MA), 300.00 (psychological level), 278.27 (200-day MA)

400.00 (psychological level)

Alphabet Daily Chart, April 28, 2026

Bottom line

Alphabet finds itself in a "show me" period like many tech and AI heavyweights. The company has the infrastructure and the strategic wins, but it must now prove that its AI-first transformation is accretive to the bottom line.

As we move toward Wednesday’s close, the focus won't just be on how much money Google made but on how efficiently they are spending it to secure the next decade of dominance.

Original Post

data.title: Veradermics: Positive VDPHL01 Data Strengthens Late-Stage Pipeline Thesis
data.description:
Thesis

Veradermics, Incorporated (MANE) has just announced some topline data from their lead candidate, VDPHL0. It’s a late-stage, potentially first-in-class asset in a pretty large and underserved market, as I’ll explain. What we saw in the recent Phase 2/3 readout was robust and consistent efficacy, with about 30 to 33 hairs/cm² regrowth versus just 7 hairs/cm² in the placebo group of men. There was also a rapid onset by Month 2 and strong patient-reported outcomes, which were nice to see. The safety profile was also comparable to placebo. On the back of this readout, I would see a clear pathway toward approval and later early commercialization in the male population, especially. We could see later expansion into women, providing some additional upside.

So from a stock perspective, I would be quite bullish on seeing more upward momentum leading into a potential FDA approval. The upside case here then hinges on VDPHL01 achieving meaningful penetration in the cash-pay hair loss market at decent premium pricing. This could realistically scale into several hundred million in early revenues if we see it come to market with good efficacy. In that scenario, the current valuation I outline would then begin to look justified or even rather conservative, particularly if we see the asset establish itself as the standard-of-care oral option in androgenetic alopecia.

Pipeline

Just briefly, Veradermics has a pretty highly concentrated pipeline. It would also be mainly centered around their lead asset, VDPHL01, rather than a broad multi-drug portfolio. You see, VDPHL01 is currently being evaluated across three key clinical studies, the first of which would be Study 302. This is a Phase 2/3 trial in men that has just reported positive topline data this week. Study 304 is a fully enrolled confirmatory Phase 3 trial in men with results expected sometime in 2H26. And lastly, Study 306 is a Phase 2/3 trial in women that is still actively enrolling.

Now, among these, the male program, particularly Study 302 and the ongoing Phase 3/Study 304, is clearly closest to commercialization. It’s probably considered the most advanced and, so far, the most de-risked program. It’s also pretty closely aligned with regulatory submission pathways, as I'll explain. So if the upcoming Phase 3 data from Study 304 confirms the strong efficacy and safety we’ve just seen in Study 302, then I would see VDPHL01 as having a clear move toward approval as the company’s first commercial product. This would make it the central value driver across the entire pipeline and what we should base our valuations on here.

VDPHL01 Topline Data

As you probably saw, Veradermics has just released some topline data for VDPHL01, which at first glance looks quite impressive. Just as a bit of background, VDPHL01 is an investigational oral therapy being developed by Veradermics for the treatment of androgenetic alopecia/pattern hair loss. The candidate itself is actually an extended-release formulation of minoxidil, which is a pretty well-established vasodilator that has long been used topically for hair growth. It’s also been used orally in a few different contexts for things like blood pressure control. So there is plenty of supporting research for minoxidil available to lean on.

Now, mechanistically speaking, minoxidil is said to promote hair growth by enhancing the body’s blood flow to the hair follicles. It sounds simple in principle, but what this specifically does is prolong the anagen/growth phase of the hair cycle and stimulate a lot of follicular activity at the cellular level. It’s also worth noting that what differentiates VDPHL01 from traditional approaches is its oral, once- or possibly twice-daily dosing. Veradermics is also combining it with a proprietary extended-release gel matrix, which is more designed towards steady systemic exposure. So this approach is tipped to avoid the high peak drug concentrations associated with immediate-release oral minoxidil. We’ve typically seen that high concentrations have been quite limited due to their cardiovascular side effects. As of now, the candidate is currently in late-stage development, with this current readout coming from a combined Phase 2/3 trial. We should view the overall setup as being pretty close to a potential regulatory submission if the subsequent studies confirm the findings.

As for the newly announced data, I think the readout is rather notable in terms of magnitude but also for the consistency of effect seen. The company took just over 500 men with mild-to-moderate hair loss, and VDPHL01 actually met all primary and key secondary endpoints with pretty high statistical significance. The participants experienced mean increases in non-vellus hair count of about 30 to 33 hairs/cm² at just six months, compared with just over 7 hairs/cm² in the placebo group. This was roughly a fourfold improvement.

Importantly, here, the efficacy wasn’t just statistically significant but also clinically meaningful and rapid. We saw separation from placebo emerging as early as two months in the study, which was a good sign. The patient-reported outcomes also reinforced this, with up to about 86% of the participants reporting improvement and a substantial proportion rating their results as improved or much improved, which far exceeded the placebo group. In terms of safety, there was also another critical differentiator, with adverse event rates being comparable to placebo. In that sense, there were no treatment-related serious adverse events or cardiac safety signals.

As I mentioned before, historically, this has been a major concern for oral minoxidil. So if we take all this together, we have a combination of strong efficacy, early onset, quite high patient satisfaction, and a placebo-like safety profile. This is what made the readout particularly compelling and translated into some pretty healthy stock momentum. I also see the key differentiating factor for VDPHL01 here as its optimized pharmacokinetic design in the sense that it managed to deliver consistent, lower-peak exposure. This seemed to unlock the benefits of systemic minoxidil while minimizing the unwanted risks. It makes the bull case scenario attractive, potentially enabling the first widely acceptable, FDA-approved oral non-hormonal therapy for pattern hair loss in decades.

A Word on the TAM

When it comes to the TAM for VDPHL01, we have to consider what this candidate will specifically and most likely be used for first. As of now, it seems unusually large for a single dermatology asset, but this would also be somewhat expected given how prevalent androgenetic alopecia is. We also need to keep in mind that this is a use case in which we often see a cash-pay nature of treatment. If we start with the U.S., there are roughly 80 million people, about 50 million men and 30 million women, who are affected.

Globally speaking, this would represent one of the largest aesthetic medicine markets, with projections to exceed about $30 billion by 2028. However, if we take into account the commercially addressable TAM at a potential first launch, I see it being far narrower. I think the initial focus will almost certainly be on male patients with mild-to-moderate hair loss, which is the population we’re seeing studied in Phase 3. This is particularly true for those already seeking treatment and dissatisfied with current options. So, it’s somewhat of a treated and motivated segment, which most likely puts the TAM in the low tens of millions globally, but only a fraction of that group will look to actively pay for therapy. For me, this represents the most realistic early revenue pool. Of course, over time, I see potential expansion into women, which is a major unmet need with fewer effective options, and also some earlier-stage patients could help meaningfully broaden the TAM.

As for the current competition, it’s somewhat fragmented and relatively weak, but this is actually pretty central to the opportunity here. The mainstays, as you know, would be topical minoxidil OTC products like Rogaine. There are also some oral finasteride products, along with off-label low-dose oral minoxidil. These would generally be pretty inexpensive. I think topical minoxidil often costs in the $10 to $30 per month range, whilst the generic finasteride offerings can usually be picked up for less than $10 a month. But with that kind of low price, they’re getting low efficacy, and as a result, adherence is usually poor. There are also some side effects, especially with finasteride, that limit uptake. More recently, however, we’ve seen a lot of telehealth-driven subscription models focus on bundled hair loss treatments. They know the value of the TAM and, more so, the willingness of a lot of men to pay up for results. This has pushed pricing into the $30 to $80 a month range, so patients are clearly willing to pay up for convenience and perceived efficacy.

As for VDPHL01, I see it positioned more as a premium, branded, FDA-approved oral therapy. If we see it come to market, I would expect Veradermics to focus more on the superior efficacy and safety aspects to differentiate from the competition. So, it could be the case that we see the offering reasonably command pricing in the $75 to $150/month range, which would give us about $900 to $1,800 annually per patient. And keep in mind that this would be expected to come in the form of a cash-pay model similar to a lot of other lifestyle-driven therapeutics.

So if we put all this together, I see a realistic commercial scenario here taking place as follows. We can assume an initial treated population of 2 to 3 million patients globally, which is a small fraction of the total prevalence, and a penetration rate of about 5% to 10% within that actively treated segment over time. This would then give us about 100,000 to 300,000 patients on therapy, and at about $1,200 annual pricing, this translates to roughly $120 million to $360 million in annual revenue in early commercialization for that conservative scenario I outlined. Of course, a much more bullish scenario would see VDPHL01 expand into women and capture a larger share of the dissatisfied treatment base.

Valuation and Risks

The valuation for Veradermics here would be a bit different since the company is still very much a development-stage biotech. Lots of metrics currently screen as not meaningful as they’re not yet generating meaningful revenue or earnings. Instead, investors here would be best served to value the company on future potential rather than current financials. On that note, they hold a market cap of about $3.7 billion and an enterprise value near the $3.9 billion mark. Now, despite a minimal revenue contribution today, I think the valuation more so reflects some expectations tied almost entirely to the commercial success of VDPHL01. It’s their lead asset and the one closest to reaching commercialization. As for the balance sheet, there is relatively low debt and a hefty $142 million cash sum, which would be pretty typical for a late-stage biotech preparing for potential commercialization but still quite reliant on capital markets.

Just to touch on their cash position briefly, we also just saw Verdermics announce a public offering. The offering should see their cash position rise from that $142 million figure to roughly $450 million in pro forma. But that's if we assume $315 million in gross proceeds. If we put that against an FY25 operating spend of about $72 million, it would then give us an updated cash runway of around six years if we see spending stay in line with last year. However, I think we can expect spending to rise going forward, but it still gives the company a pretty strong cushion to fund Phase 3 completion.

What I see the market is pricing in here is a high probability of regulatory approval, followed by meaningful commercial uptake. Also, that uptake would be assumed to be in a pretty large, underserved market like androgenetic alopecia. And given the strong Phase 2/3 data, investors seem to be discounting forward to a scenario in which we see VDPHL01 become a first-in-class oral, non-hormonal therapy with some blockbuster potential. The absence of current revenue metrics here would also seem to shift the market focus to long-term peak sales assumptions and market penetration, as I mentioned. This will also come down to differentiation versus existing treatments. Now, at the same time, we have to mention that this valuation implicitly carries some execution risk. Any setbacks in Phase 3, or the regulatory review to come, or even commercialization, could materially impact the stock here, simply because there is very little in the way of diversified revenue streams to support any downside. So, in short, Veradermics is being valued less like a traditional healthcare company and more so like a binary, late-stage asset play for a lot of investors. Their future cash flows, rather than present fundamentals, are driving investor expectations.

Looking Ahead

Just to conclude, the story around Veradermics here would ultimately come down to execution on VDPHL01. This recent data definitely reinforces the view that this is a pretty differentiated, late-stage asset with strong efficacy. We also have quite a rapid onset and a clean safety profile, which would arguably address a lot of the key gaps in today’s treatment landscape. I think if the upcoming Phase 3 readout confirms these results and we also see the company can successfully move toward approval and commercialization, there could be more positive stock momentum ahead. There's a clear path for meaningful revenue generation in a pretty large, cash-pay market. So in that context, the current valuation would then start to look increasingly tied to a rather realistic upside scenario, in which we see VDPHL01 establish itself as a leading oral option.
